questions.text: Receptor sites for neurotransmitters are present on the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Presynaptic membrane has synaptic vesicles which contain neurotransmitter. When nerve impulse reaches the vesicles, neurotransmitter is released into the synaptic cleft.
Tips of axons have synaptic vesicles, which contain neurotransmitter.

A neurotransmitter is a chemical substance responsible for the transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse. It is released by the synaptic vesicle into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds with protein receptor molecule present on the postsynaptic membrane causing its depolarization and generation of an action potential.
Synaptic vesicles contain neurotransmitter for transmission of impulse.

questions.explanation: A neurotransmitter is a chemical substance responsible for the transmission of nerve impulses across a synapse. It is released by the synaptic vesicle into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds with protein receptor molecule present on the postsynaptic membrane causing its depolarization and generation of an action potential.

questions.text: Which of the following regions of the brain is incorrectly paired with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Corpus callosum - communication between the left and right cerebral cortices
options.explanation: The corpus callosum facilitates communication between the left and right hemispheres of the brain, allowing for coordinated functions.
The cerebrum is responsible for higher cognitive functions, including calculation, contemplation, and other complex thought processes.
The medulla oblongata plays a crucial role in maintaining homeostasis by regulating vital functions such as heart rate and breathing.
The cerebellum is primarily involved in motor control and coordination, not language comprehension, which is a function of the cerebrum. This makes it the incorrect pairing.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates movement and balance. Language comprehension is a function of the cerebrum.

questions.text: Injury localized to the hypothalamus would most likely disrupt:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Short-term memory is mainly associated with the hippocampus in the temporal lobe, not the hypothalamus.

Coordination during locomotion is controlled mainly by the cerebellum, not the hypothalamus. The cerebellum integrates sensory input with motor commands to maintain balance, posture, and smooth, coordinated movements while walking or running.

options.text: Executive functions; such as decision making
options.explanation: Executive functions such as decision-making, planning, and judgment are primarily controlled by the frontal lobe of the cerebrum, especially the prefrontal cortex. The hypothalamus, on the other hand, mainly regulates autonomic functions like hunger, thirst, temperature, and hormone release, not higher executive tasks.

The hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus. It provides an anatomical connection between the nervous and endocrine systems through its relationship to the pituitary gland. The hypothalamus is a thermoregulatory centre. Hence, it is called the “thermostat” of the body. It keeps body temperature at roughly 37°C by means of a complex thermostat system. Any localised injury to the hypothalamus will, hence, disrupt the regulation of body temperature.

questions.explanation: The hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus. It provides an anatomical connection between the nervous and endocrine systems through its relationship to the pituitary gland. The hypothalamus is a thermoregulatory centre. Hence, it is called the “thermostat” of the body. It keeps body temperature at roughly 37°C by means of a complex thermostat system. Any localised injury to the hypothalamus will, hence, disrupt the regulation of body temperature.

questions.text: The human hind brain comprises of three parts, one of which is the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The spinal cord is part of the central nervous system but is not a component of the hindbrain; it extends from the base of the brain down through the vertebral column.
The corpus callosum connects the left and right cerebral hemispheres, but it is part of the forebrain, not the hindbrain.
The cerebellum is a key component of the hindbrain, responsible for coordination of voluntary movements, posture, and balance.
The hypothalamus is located in the forebrain and plays a role in regulating various autonomic functions such as temperature control, hunger, and hormonal balance.

questions.explanation: The hindbrain has pons, cerebellum, and medulla oblongata. Cerebellum coordinates voluntary movements, posture, and balance.

questions.text: Which part of human brain is concerned with the regulation of body temperature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Your cerebellum is a part of your brain located at the back of your head, just above and behind where your spinal cord connects to your brain itself. It is involved in muscular coordination,balance and position of body in space.

Cerebrum, the largest and uppermost portion of the brain. The cerebrum consists of the cerebral hemispheres and accounts for two-thirds of the total weight of the brain. One hemisphere, usually the left, is functionally dominant, controlling language and speech.
Your hypothalamus, a structure deep in your brain, acts as your body's smart control coordinating center. Its main function is to keep your body in a stable state called homeostasis.
Medulla oblongata is the reflex center of vital tasks like heart beat, breathing, sneezing, vomiting and coughing.

questions.explanation: Your hypothalamus, a structure deep in your brain, acts as your body's smart control coordinating center. Its main function is to keep your body in a stable state called homeostasis.The hypothalamus controls body temperature by acting like a thermostat, receiving information from heat and cold sensors throughout the body to maintain a core temperature of about 37°C (98.6°F). If the body is too cold, it triggers mechanisms like vasoconstriction, shivering, and increased metabolism to generate and retain heat. If the body is too hot, it activates cooling responses such as vasodilation (widening of skin blood vessels) and sweating to release heat into the environment.

questions.text: During the transmission of nerve impulse through a nerve fibre,the potential on the inner side of the plasma membrane has which type of electric charge?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: First positive, then negative and continues to be negative.
options.explanation: First positive, then negative, and continues to be negative — The membrane starts depolarized, then becomes repolarized and stays polarized.

options.text: First negative then positive and continues to be positive
options.explanation: First negative then positive and continues to be positive – The resting negative potential is reversed and maintained as a prolonged depolarized state.

options.text: First positive then negative and again back to positive
options.explanation: First positive then negative and again back to positive – The membrane potential oscillates, starts from depolarized state, repolarizing, then depolarizing again.

options.text: First negative then positive and again back to negative
options.explanation: A nerve is a strand of tissue comprising many nerve fibres plus supporting tissue enclosed in a connective tissue sheath. The nerve impulse is the signal that travels along the length of a nerve fibre. This is how information is transmitted through the nervous system. It is marked by the flow of ions across the membrane of the axon caused by changes in the permeability of the membrane, producing a reduction in the potential difference that can be detected as the action potential. The strength of the impulse produced in any nerve fibre is constant.

questions.explanation: A nerve is a strand of tissue comprising many nerve fibres plus supporting tissue enclosed in a connective tissue sheath. The nerve impulse is the signal that travels along the length of a nerve fibre. This is how information is transmitted through the nervous system. It is marked by the flow of ions across the membrane of the axon caused by changes in the permeability of the membrane, producing a reduction in the potential difference that can be detected as the action potential. The strength of the impulse produced in any nerve fibre is constant.

questions.text: Which one of the following does not act as a neurotransmitter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cortisone is a pregnene steroid hormone. It is a naturally-occurring corticosteroid metabolite that is also used as a pharmaceutical prodrug.

Acetylcholine is an organic compound that functions in the brain and body of many types of animals as a neurotransmitter. Its name is derived from its chemical structure: it is an ester of acetic acid and choline. Parts in the body that use or are affected by acetylcholine are referred to as cholinergic.

Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone and medication which is involved in regulating visceral functions. It appears as a white microcrystalline granule. Adrenaline is normally produced by the adrenal glands and by a small number of neurons in the medulla oblongata.
Norepinephrine, also called noradrenaline or noradrenalin, is an organic chemical in the catecholamine family that functions in the brain and body as both a hormone and neurotransmitter.

questions.explanation: Neurotransmitters are chemicals that are used to relay, amplify, and modulate electrical signals between a neuron and another cell. Substances that act as neurotransmitters can be categorized into three major groups: (1) amino acids (primarily glutamic acid, GABA, aspartic acid, and glycine), (2) peptides (vasopressin, somatostatin, neurotensin, etc.), and (3) monoamines (norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin) plus acetylcholine. Cortisone is a glucocorticoid steroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands and has anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressant properties.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct for the the node of Ranvier of the nerve?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neurilemma is the membrane of the nerve and provides a base for myelin sheaths and nodes of ranvier. However neurilemma is not continuous.

At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

options.text: Both neurilemma and myelin sheath are discontinuous.
options.explanation: Incorrect because neurilemma is not discontinuous. It is the covering of the nerve membrane. But myelin sheath is discontinuous as nerve impulse needs to be propagated through nodes of ranvier.

options.text: It is covered by the myelin sheath
options.explanation: It is not totally covered by myelin sheath, instead is discontinuous and contains nodes of ranvier through which nerve impulses travel.

questions.explanation: At the level of the node of Ranvier, the myelin sheath is discontinuous but not the neurilemma lining. Actually, the myelin sheath is an integral part of the Schwann cell – which forms a continuous neurilemmal covering. Each Schwann cell wraps around the neurite to form concentric layers of the plasma membrane. But at the level of the junction between two Schwann cells, myelin cannot be formed and thus a gap appears.

questions.text: The junction between the axon of one neuron and the dendrite of the next is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A constant bridge is not a recognized term in neuroscience. It suggests a stable connection, which does not apply to the dynamic interaction between neurons.
While this term indicates a meeting place, it lacks the specificity needed in neuroanatomy. The proper term for this connection is different and more widely accepted.
A joint typically refers to a connection between bones in the skeletal system. It is not applicable when discussing the interaction between neurons.
A synapse is the correct term that describes the junction where the axon terminal of one neuron meets the dendrite of another, allowing for signal transmission through neurotransmitter release.

questions.explanation: A synapse is the functional junction between two neurons separated by a tiny synaptic cleft (-200 A). Neurotransmitters released from the presynaptic membrane cross this gap to transmit the nerve impulse to the postsynaptic neuron.

questions.text: Which of the following is regarded as a unit of nervous tissue?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neurons are the fundamental units of the nervous system, responsible for transmitting information throughout the body. They consist of a cell body that contains the nucleus and cytoplasm, along with specialized structures like axons and dendrites that facilitate communication between neurons.

The myelin sheath is a protective covering that surrounds some axons, formed by glial cells. While it plays a crucial role in speeding up nerve impulse transmission, it is not a unit of nervous tissue itself.

Axons are long, slender projections of neurons that conduct electrical impulses away from the cell body. Although axons are essential components of neurons, they do not represent a complete unit of nervous tissue on their own.

Dendrites are tree-like structures that receive signals from other neurons and transmit them to the cell body. While they are critical for neuronal function, they are part of a neuron, not a distinct unit of nervous tissue.

questions.explanation: Neurons are the structural and functional units of the nervous system. Each neuron consists of a cell body, a xon, sendritas, specialized for receiving and transmitting nerve impulses. The myelin sheath only insulates some axons but is not itself a unit of nervous tissue.

questions.text: One function of the parasympathetic nervous system is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The contraction of hair muscles, also known as arrector pili muscles, is primarily regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. It causes hair to stand on end, a response known as piloerection.

Sweat gland activity is mainly regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic system promotes the production and release of sweat in response to heat or stress, not the parasympathetic system.
The acceleration of the heart rate is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. It triggers the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate to prepare the body for action.

This is the correct answer. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for constricting the pupil (making it smaller) in response to non-stressful, restful situations. This action allows for better near vision and reduces the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.explanation: The action of the parasympathetic nervous system is opposite to that of the sympathetic nervous system. If the sympathetic nervous system accelerates an action, the parasympathetic nervous system slows it. However, neither system is exclusively excitatory or inhibitory. The parasympathetic fibres constrict the pupil, decrease the rate and force of heartbeat, dilate many blood vessels, lower the arterial blood pressure, quicken the peristaltic movements, and contract the urinary bladder.

questions.text: GnRH a hypothalamic hormone, needed in reproduction acts on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Anterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and FSH
options.explanation: GnRH (Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone) is indeed a hypothalamic hormone involved in reproduction. It primarily acts on the anterior pituitary gland.GnRH stimulates the secretion of LH (Luteinizing Hormone) and FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) from the anterior pituitary gland.

options.text: Posterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of oxytocin and FSH
options.explanation: GnRH does not directly stimulate the posterior pituitary gland. FSH secretion is primarily regulated by GnRH and occurs in the anterior pituitary gland, not the posterior pituitary gland. Oxytocin is released from the posterior pituitary gland, but GnRH does not stimulate its secretion.

options.text: Posterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and relaxin
options.explanation: GnRH typically does not stimulate the posterior pituitary gland. Relaxin is not a hormone typically stimulated by GnRH. Also, LH and relaxin are not the hormones of posterior pituitary.

options.text: Anterior pituitary gland and stimulates secretion of LH and oxytocin.
options.explanation: GnRH acts on the anterior pituitary gland. It stimulates the secretion of LH and FSH, not oxytocin. Oxytocin is found in the posterior pituitary.

questions.explanation: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) is secreted by the hypothalamus, which stimulates the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland to secrete luteinising hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH). Both these hormones are involved in the reproductive cycle of females. FSH stimulates the follicles to mature, and LH is involved in ovulation and the formation of the corpus luteum.Other options are mismatched.

questions.text: Hypersecretion of growth hormone in adults does not cause further increase in height because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Epiphyseal plates are areas of cartilage at the ends of long bones where growth occurs. They typically close or ossify after adolescence, which means they turn into bone and no longer allow for further increase in height.

options.text: Bones loose their sensitivity to growth hormone in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. While it's true that the growth plates close after adolescence, it's not because bones lose their sensitivity to growth hormone. Instead, it's due to the natural developmental process.

options.text: Muscle fibres do not grow in size after birth
options.explanation: This statement is generally true. Muscle fibers can grow in size through hypertrophy, but this process is not primarily regulated by growth hormone. It's not directly related to height increase.

options.text: Growth hormone becomes inactive in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Growth hormone remains active in adults, although its role shifts from primarily promoting longitudinal bone growth (height) during childhood and adolescence to other functions in adulthood, such as maintaining muscle mass and bone density.

questions.explanation: Epiphyseal plate is a hyaline cartilage plate in the metaphysis at each end of long bone. It is part of long bone where new bone growth takes place until puberty .When the plate ossifies and fuses into the epiphyseal line the growth of bone stops. In adults, elevated levels of GH results in acromegaly where no increase in height occurs because of ossified epiphyseal plate .Instead bones grow in thickness.

questions.text: A temporary endocrine gland in the human body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The corpus cardiacum is a structure found in insects, not in humans. It's part of the insect endocrine system and is responsible for storing and releasing neurohormones.

The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland in the human body. It forms in the ovary after ovulation when a mature follicle transforms into a structure that produces hormones, including progesterone, to support early pregnancy. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum breaks down.
The corpus allatum is also a structure found in insects and not in humans. It's part of the insect endocrine system and is involved in producing juvenile hormones that influence insect development.

The pineal gland is a small endocrine gland in the human brain. While it's not typically described as "temporary," it plays a crucial role in regulating the sleep-wake cycle and producing the hormone melatonin, which is involved in regulating sleep and circadian rhythms.

questions.explanation: The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine gland in human females. It is formed after the follicle ruptures and LH transforms this ruptured follicle into a yellow glandular mass called the corpus luteum. It secretes progesterone, which stimulates the uterine glands to produce an increased amount of watery mucus and is also essential for the maintenance of the endometrium lining. In the absence of fertilization, the corpus luteum degenerates, leading to menstruation. Hence, it is considered a temporary gland because it secretes hormones for some time and then degenerates.

questions.text: Graves’ disease is caused due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyposecretion means the thyroid gland is not producing enough thyroid hormones. Graves' disease is actually characterized by hypersecretion (overproduction) of thyroid hormones, not hyposecretion.

This is the correct answer. Graves' disease is caused by an autoimmune response where the immune system stimulates the thyroid gland to produce excessive amounts of thyroid hormones, leading to hyperthyroidism.
Adrenal gland hyposecretion is unrelated to Graves' disease. The adrenal glands primarily produce hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, not thyroid hormones.

Adrenal gland hypersecretion is also unrelated to Graves' disease. An overactive adrenal gland might result in excessive production of adrenal hormones, but this condition is distinct from Graves' disease.

questions.explanation: Graves' disease is characterized by secretion of excessive amount of thyroid hormone (T4 and T3). It is an autoimmune disease. Thyroid hormones affect many organs in the body. So Graves' disease symptoms also can affect those organs. Anyone can get Graves' disease. But it's more common in women and in people older than 30. It is characterized by exophthalmia (protrusion of eye balls because of fluid accumulation behind them), loss of weight, slight rise in the body temperature, excitability, rapid heart beat, nervousness and restlessness.

questions.text: Name a peptide hormone which acts mainly on hepatocytes, adipocytes and enhances cellular glucose uptake and utilization:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin is produced by the pancreas and plays a crucial role in regulating blood sugar levels. It promotes glucose uptake by cells, including hepatocytes (liver cells) and adipocytes (fat cells), facilitating glucose utilization and storage.

Glucagon is another hormone produced by the pancreas, but it has the opposite effect of insulin. It raises blood sugar levels by promoting the release of glucose from the liver. It does not enhance cellular glucose uptake.

Secretin is a hormone produced by the small intestine that primarily stimulates the pancreas to release bicarbonate, aiding in neutralizing stomach acid. It does not have a direct role in glucose uptake or utilization.
Gastrin is produced by the stomach and plays a role in regulating stomach acid secretion. It is not involved in glucose metabolism or cellular glucose uptake.

questions.explanation: Insulin is a peptide hormone, secreted by the b cells of the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas, that promotes the uptake of glucose by body cells, particularly in the liver (hepatocytes) and muscles (adipocytes), and thereby controls its concentration in the blood. In these tissues, the absorbed glucose is converted into either glycogen, via glycogenesis, or fats (triglycerides), via lipogenesis; in the liver, glucose is converted into both.

questions.text: The posterior pituitary gland is not a ‘true’ endocrine gland because :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is provided with a duct
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The posterior lobe of the pituitary gland (neurohypophysis) is a collection of nerve endings and axon terminals from the hypothalamus, not a duct, which stores and releases hormones like oxytocin and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) into the bloodstream.

options.text: It only stores and releases hormones
options.explanation: This statement i correct. The posterior pituitary gland primarily stores and releases hormones produced by the hypothalamus, such as oxytocin and vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone). It does not produce hormones on its own.

options.text: It is under the regulation of hypothalamus
options.explanation: This statement is accurate but it is valid for the whole pituitary gland not just the posterior pituitary . As the whole pituitary gland secretions is controlled by hypothalamus . It is not the reason that why posterior pituitary is not a true endocrine gland.
This statement is incorrect. The posterior pituitary gland does not secrete enzymes; its main function is to store and release hormones.

questions.explanation: The posterior pituitary is not a true endocrine gland because it does not produce its own hormones. Instead, it functions as a storage area for hormones, specifically antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin, which are synthesized in the hypothalamus. These hormones are transported down nerve fibers from the hypothalamus to the posterior pituitary, where they are then released into the bloodstream when stimulated.

questions.text: Which one of the following hormones is not involved in sugar metabolism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas, and it plays a crucial role in sugar metabolism. Its main function is to lower blood sugar levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose into cells, where it can be used for energy or stored as glycogen.

Glucagon is another hormone produced by the pancreas, but it has the opposite effect of insulin. Glucagon raises blood sugar levels by promoting the breakdown of glycogen into glucose in the liver and stimulating the release of glucose into the bloodstream.

Cortisone is a corticosteroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands. While cortisone does have various metabolic effects, it primarily regulates inflammation and has a relatively minor role in sugar metabolism compared to insulin and glucagon.

Aldosterone is also a hormone produced by the adrenal glands, but it primarily regulates salt and water balance in the body, particularly in the kidneys. It does not have a direct role in sugar metabolism.

questions.explanation: Aldosterone (salt retaining hormone) is the principal mineralocorticoid in humans, secreted by adrenal cortex. Its main function is to regulate sodium content of the body.

questions.text: Which one of the following hormones is released by the posterior part of pituitary gland?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prolactin is not released by the posterior pituitary but by the anterior pituitary gland. It primarily stimulates milk production in the mammary glands.
MSH is produced by cells in the anterior pituitary and not the posterior pituitary. It plays a role in regulating skin pigmentation.

This is the correct answer. ADH is produced by the neurons in the posterior pituitary. It regulates water balance in the body by influencing the reabsorption of water in the kidneys, thereby reducing urine production and helping to maintain blood pressure.

LH is also released by the anterior pituitary, not the posterior pituitary. In males, LH stimulates the production of testosterone, and in females, it triggers ovulation and supports the formation of the corpus luteum.

questions.explanation: Two hormones, i.e oxytocin (OT) and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) are synthesised in the hypothalamus, but stored and released by the posterior lobe of pituitary gland. Hypothalamus is regarded as the Master Gland.

questions.text: A pregnant female delivers a baby who suffers from stunted growth, mental retardation, a low intelligence quotient and abnormal skin.This is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thyroid cancer typically leads to an overproduction of thyroid hormones rather than a deficiency. Therefore, it is not likely to be the cause of the described symptoms.

The pars distalis is a part of the anterior pituitary gland that secretes various hormones, but it does not produce thyroid hormones directly. Oversecretion of hormones from the pars distalis could lead to other issues, but is not directly related to the symptoms described.

This is the correct answer. Iodine is an essential nutrient required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones (T3 and T4). A deficiency of iodine in the diet can lead to hypothyroidism, particularly in pregnant women, which can result in the described symptoms in the newborn. This condition is known as congenital hypothyroidism or cretinism.

Growth hormone deficiency can result in stunted growth, but it is not typically associated with the specific combination of symptoms mentioned, such as mental retardation, low intelligence quotient, and abnormal skin.

questions.explanation: Iodine is needed for the synthesis of T3 and T4. Iodine binds to the tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin, which is then hydrolysed into iodotyrosines that combine to form tri-iodothyronine (T3 ) or thyroxine (tetraiodothyronine or T4 ). Therefore, a deficiency of iodine in the diet of a pregnant female will lead to the improper synthesis of thyroid hormones in the newborn infant.

questions.text: Norepinephrine (i)is released by sympathetic fibers (ii)is released by parasympathetic fibers (iii)increases the heart rate (iv)decreases blood pressureWhich of the above statements are correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sympathetic fibers are also called small-fiber nerves. It is developed from the collections of nerve clusters called ganglia. It is available on the outside of the spinal cord. It will increase the rate of heartbeat, muscle contraction, and rate of conduction. So, i is correct. And iii is also correct.

The parasympathetic nervous system consists of cranial nerves, the vagus nerve, and the lumbar spinal nerves. During this nerve stimulation, it increases digestive secretions and also reduces heart rate. The norepinephrine hormone will increase the heartbeat. So, ii is not correct and iii is correct.
Sympathetic fibers release the norepinephrine. Blood pressure is increased during the large amount of norepinephrine hormone excretion. So, i is correct but iv is not the correct answer.
Parasympathetic fibers do not produce the norepinephrine hormone. Decreasing blood pressure is not possible during the excretion of hormones. So, both ii and iv are not correct.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is secreted by some neurons of the sympathetic nervous system and also by the adrenal medulla. It accelerates the heart rate. Norepinephrine is a hormone and neurotransmitter that primarily functions to mediate the body's "fight-or-flight" response to stress by increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and blood flow to muscles and the brain. It also plays a crucial role in the brain by regulating attention, focus, alertness, and the sleep-wake cycle. Additionally, it affects mood and memory and is involved in energy regulation through the breakdown of stored fats and glycogen for glucose.

questions.text: A person entering an empty room suddenly finds a snake right in front of him.Which one of the following is likely to happen in his neuro­hormonal control system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla
options.explanation: The correct answer is: The sympathetic nervous system is activated, releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla—this triggers the “fight-or-flight” response for immediate action.

options.text: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly across the cleft and transmit a nerve impulse.
options.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly… – True in general nerve transmission, but not the main event in this sudden danger scenario.

options.text: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division of brain
options.explanation: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division… – Parasympathetic is for “rest-and-digest,” not emergency response.

options.text: Sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal cortex.
options.explanation: Sympathetic nervous system… from the adrenal cortex – Adrenal cortex releases steroid hormones (like cortisol, aldosterone), not epinephrine/norepinephrine.

questions.explanation: Hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine are secreted from the adrenal medulla. They are emergency hormones released in conditions of stress, emergency, etc. Epinephrine and norepinephrine are also released by adrenergic nerve fibres of the sympathetic nervous system, where they act as neurotransmitters.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs of hormones are the examples of those that can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it(mostly in the nucleus)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Both insulin and glucagon are peptide hormones that cannot easily pass through the cell membrane. They bind to receptors on the surface of the target cell.
While thyroxine can pass through the cell membrane, insulin is a peptide hormone that binds to cell surface receptors, making this pair incorrect.
Both somatostatin and oxytocin are peptide hormones that bind to receptors on the cell surface rather than passing through the cell membrane.
Cortisol and testosterone are steroid hormones that can easily pass through the cell membrane and bind to intracellular receptors, often located in the nucleus.

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones such as cortisol, testosterone, estradiol, and progesterone mostly regulate gene expression or chromosome function by the interaction of the hormone-receptor complex with the genome. So, these can easily pass through the cell membrane of the target cell and bind to a receptor inside it.

questions.text: Low Ca2+ in the body fluid may be the cause of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Low levels of blood Ca++ ions stimulate the parathyroid directly to increase parathormone production, whereas high levels of Ca++ ions suppress its release. Underactivity causes a drop in blood Ca++ ions, which in turn leads to muscular tetany.

Anemia is a condition in which the number of red blood cells (RBCs) or the amount of hemoglobin in them is lower than normal, reducing the blood’s capacity to carry oxygen. This results in symptoms such as fatigue, weakness, pale skin, dizziness, and shortness of breath.
Angina pectoris—or simply angina—is chest pain or discomfort that keeps coming back.

Gout is a common and complex form of arthritis that can affect anyone. It's characterized by sudden, severe attacks of pain, swelling, redness, and tenderness in one or more joints, most often in the big toe.

questions.explanation: Tetany is a spasm and twitching of the muscles, particularly those of the face, hands, and feet. Tetany is usually caused by a reduction in the blood calcium level, which may be due to underactive parathyroid glands, rickets, or alkalosis.

questions.text: The blood calcium level is lowered by the deficiency of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parathormone and calcitonin are antagonistic to each other.

A lack of calcitonin in the blood may increase a person's risk of bone degradation and osteopenia, a condition that occurs when the rate at which the body reabsorbs old bone exceeds the rate at which it produces new bone.
Hypoparathyroidism occurs when the glands produce too little PTH. The blood calcium level falls, and the phosphorus level rises.
In adults, thyroxine deficiency will lower the metabolic rate, causing weight gain, memory problems, infertility, fatigue, and muscle stiffness.

questions.explanation: Parathormone is secreted by chief cells of the parathyroid that regulate the metabolism of calcium and phosphate. It increases calcium absorption from the intestine and also increases calcium resorption from the nephrons of the kidneys. So its deficiency leads to low blood calcium level.

questions.text: A person is having problems with calcium and phosphorus metabolism in his body. Which one of the following glands may not be functioning properly?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The parotid glands contribute a watery, proteinaceous secretion to whole saliva.

This is the first section of the small intestine. The main hormones secreted by the endocrine gland in the pancreas are insulin and glucagon, which regulate the level of glucose in the blood, and somatostatin, which prevents the release of insulin and glucagon.
The thyroid gland produces hormones that regulate the body's metabolic rate, growth and development.

Parathormone is secreted by chief cells of the parathyroid that regulates the metabolism of calcium and phosphate. It increases calcium absorption from the intestine and also increases calcium resorption from the nephrons of the kidneys. So its deficiency leads to low blood calcium level.

questions.explanation: Parathormone is secreted by chief cells of the parathyroid that regulates the metabolism of calcium and phosphate. So its deficiency leads to low blood calcium level . Parathormone, also known as parathyroid hormone (PTH), is a hormone produced by the parathyroid glands in the neck that regulates blood calcium levels. It increases calcium in the blood by releasing calcium from bones, increasing calcium absorption in the intestines (via active vitamin D), and reducing calcium loss in the kidneys. When blood calcium levels drop, PTH is released to restore them, and an imbalance in PTH can lead to conditions associated with abnormal calcium levels.

questions.text: Mainly which type of hormones control the menstrual cycle in human beings?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The menstrual cycle is controlled by several endocrine parameters. At the beginning of the cycle, FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) of the pituitary initiates the development of an ovarian follicle. A growing ovarian follicle gradually secretes an increasing amount of estrogen. This in turn leads to a sudden surge of LH secretion by the pituitary. As the LH (luteinising hormone) level in blood suddenly increases there is ovulation. Thus, only FSH or LH cannot control all the events of the menstrual cycle. Progesterone is released by the corpus luteum after ovulation, which actually prepares the uterus for a possible pregnancy. If there is no fertilisation, the progesterone level falls and there is the beginning of a new cycle.

questions.text: MSH is secreted by the

options.explanation: The anterior lobe of the pituitary secretes FSH, LH, TSH, ACTH, and STH. Many of these control the secretions from other glands, which is why it is known as the master gland of the endocrine system.

The middle lobe of the pituitary secretes a hormone named melanocyte-stimulating hormone. It stimulates the synthesis of the black pigment melanin in the skin, and also causes dispersal of melanin granules in the pigment cells, thereby darkening the colour of the skin.

The posterior lobe of the pituitary secretes oxytocin and vasopressin. Both of these are synthesized in the hypothalamus and are stored in the posterior lobe of the pituitary; from here, they are released upon stimulation.

questions.text: Secretion of progesterone by corpus luteum is initiated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Testosterone is a hormone that is primarily produced in the testicles in males and in smaller amounts in the ovaries in females. It plays an important role in the development of male reproductive tissues, such as the testicles and prostate gland, as well as secondary sexual characteristics such as increased muscle mass, body hair growth, and deepening of the voice. In females, testosterone also plays a role in maintaining bone density and muscle mass.

Thyroxine is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland. It plays an important role in regulating the body's metabolic rate, which affects how quickly the body uses energy and how quickly the body's cells function. Thyroxine levels can be affected by a variety of factors, including diet, stress, and certain medical conditions. If the thyroid gland produces too much or too little thyroxine, it can lead to a range of health problems, including weight gain or loss, fatigue, and other symptoms.

Melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) is a hormone that is produced by the pituitary gland in the brain. It plays an important role in regulating skin pigmentation by stimulating the production of melanin, which gives skin its color. MSH also plays a role in regulating appetite and other physiological processes.

questions.text: The gonadotrophic hormones are secreted by the

options.explanation: Gonadotrophic hormones are secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. (ii) Interstitial cell-stimulating hormone (ICSH): It activates the Leydig’s (interstitial) cells of the testis to secrete androgens. In females, it stimulates the corpus luteum of the ovary to secrete progesterone. In females, it is termed luteinizing hormone (LH).

questions.subject: Biology

Iodine deficiency causes hypothyroidism, which may lead to myxedema in adults or cretinism in infants.
Na+ deficiency doesn't cause diabetes mellitus. It is caused by the deficiency of the hormone insulin, which is secreted by the pancreas. Na+ is linked with water and solute maintenance in the body.

questions.explanation: Diabetes can be described as a hormonal deficiency when there is a lack of the hormone insulin (due to the pancreas not making enough) or when the body becomes resistant to insulin's effects, leading to high blood sugar. While there is another condition called diabetes insipidus, which is also a hormonal issue involving a deficiency of the hormone vasopressin (ADH) (a different hormone from insulin) and causes excessive thirst and urination, the term "diabetes" generally refers to the metabolic disorder of high blood sugar linked to insulin deficiency.

It is under the feedback control of plasma calcium concentration, and is secreted when the concentration of calcium rises in the blood. Calcitonin lowers blood calcium by reducing the activity of osteoclasts in bones, preventing the release of calcium into the blood, and increasing calcium excretion through the kidneys. It also reduces calcium absorption in the intestines. Released by the thyroid gland's C-cells in response to high calcium levels, calcitonin acts on bones, kidneys, and the digestive system to maintain calcium homeostasis.

questions.text: The hormone that stimulates the stomach to secrete gastric juice is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enterokinase is an enzyme that is produced in the small intestine. It helps to activate other enzymes that are involved in the digestion of proteins. Specifically, enterokinase activates an enzyme called trypsinogen, which is produced by the pancreas. Once trypsinogen is activated, it becomes trypsin, which can then break down proteins into smaller peptides and amino acids that can be absorbed into the bloodstream.

Enterogastrone is a hormone that is produced in the small intestine. It helps to regulate the digestive system by slowing down the movement of food through the stomach and small intestine. This allows more time for nutrients to be absorbed into the bloodstream.

Gastrin is a peptide hormone produced by G cells in the pyloric antrum of the stomach, duodenum, and pancreas. It is released into the bloodstream when food enters the stomach and stimulates the secretion of gastric acid, hydrochloric acid, and pepsin from the stomach lining. Gastrin also plays a role in gastric motility and in the growth and differentiation of stomach cells.

questions.text: A motor nerve carries impulses from:

options.explanation: Motor neurons are part of the spinal nerves. Spinal nerves carry the motor neurons to the effectors. This option is very vague, hence incorrect.
Effectors do not carry messages to the CNS. Rather, it is the other way around as the CNS needs to give command to the effectors(i.e. muscle or gland). Cranial nerves are part of the CNS.

There are three types of neurons:Motor neurons carry the impulse from CNS to the Effectors.Intermediate neurons connect the Sensory and motor neuron present in CNSSensory neurons carry the impulse from receptors to the CNS.Effectors are the organs that receive command from CNS to take action in response to a stimulus. questions.explanation: Motor neurons are meant to conduct messages from CNS to the effectors as they are emerging from CNS like glands or muscles and stimulates them to work (secrete hormones or contract). That is why they are known as motor neurons.Motor neurons carry electrical impulses from the Central Nervous System (CNS) to effectors (muscles or glands) by transmitting signals through their cell body, a long axon, and terminal branches to the effector's muscle fibers or gland cells. The signal travels as an electrical impulse along the axon, which may be insulated by a myelin sheath to speed up transmission. At the axon terminal, neurotransmitters are released, crossing the synapse to trigger a response in the effector, such as muscle contraction or hormone release. Hence option D is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following produce response?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Effectors are the organs, such as muscles or glands, that respond to signals from the central nervous system (CNS) to carry out actions in reaction to stimuli.
Nerves are the structures that transmit signals within the nervous system, but they do not produce a response themselves; rather, they relay information to effectors.
Stimulators refer to devices or methods used to provoke a response in the nervous system but do not themselves produce a response. They assist in the functioning of effectors.
The brain is responsible for processing information and sending commands to effectors, but it does not directly produce a response; it coordinates the response through effectors.

questions.explanation: Effectors are organs like muscles or glands that produce a response to a stimulus by contracting or secreting substances when signaled by the nervous or endocrine system.

questions.text: Myelinated, single and long fiber that takes message towards the cell body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A dendron is a long, myelinated protoplasmic extension of a neuron that conducts electrochemical signals towards the cell body. It is distinguished from dendrites, which are shorter and branch off from the dendron.
An axon is a long, myelinated nerve fiber that transmits impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors. Therefore, it does not carry messages towards the cell body.
Dendrites are short, branched extensions of a neuron that receive signals from other neurons and carry them towards the cell body. However, they are not typically classified as a single long fiber.
The soma, or cell body, is part of the neuron that contains the nucleus and organelles. It does not function as a fiber that transmits messages.

questions.explanation: Dendron is a single, long protoplasmic extension of a neuron that carries impulses toward the cell body. Smaller branches from dendron are called dendrites.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correct about myelin sheath?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The myelin sheath is indeed composed of specialized lipids, primarily sphingomyelin, that form a protective and insulating layer around the axon. This structure is crucial for its function.
The myelin sheath facilitates saltatory conduction, allowing nerve impulses to jump between nodes of Ranvier, thereby increasing the speed of impulse transmission significantly.
This statement is incorrect because the myelin sheath itself does not conduct impulses. Instead, it insulates the axon, which is where the actual conduction of impulses occurs.
This statement is true; the myelin sheath is absent at the nodes of Ranvier. These nodes are essential for the generation of new action potentials as ions flow across the membrane.

questions.explanation: The myelin sheath does not conduct impulses. It insulates the axon, speeding conduction through saltatory conduction. at nodes of Ranvier, but impulses themselves travel along the axon.

questions.text: The myelin sheath:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect because the myelin sheath is only present in myelinated axons. In contrast, unmyelinated axons do not have this sheath and therefore conduct nerve impulses at a slower rate.

This option is incorrect. Dendrites are primarily responsible for receiving signals and are typically unmyelinated. The myelin sheath is exclusive to axons, where it enhances the rapid transmission of nerve impulses.

This is the correct answer. The myelin sheath acts as an insulator for the axon, facilitating saltatory conduction, where electrical impulses jump between gaps known as the nodes of Ranvier, significantly increasing the speed of nerve impulse transmission.

While true that the myelin sheath is composed of proteins and lipids, this detail does not pertain directly to its primary role in increasing the conduction speed of nerve impulses, making this option less relevant.

questions.explanation: The myelin sheath acts as an insulator for axons, allowing impulses to jump between nodes (saltatory conduction), which increases the speed of nerve impulse transmission.

questions.text: Which of the following is most abundant type of neurons in CNS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are specialized for transmitting information from sensory organs to the CNS. They are actually the least abundant type of neuron in the CNS, accounting for approximately 10% of the total neuronal population.

Also known as interneurons, associative neurons are the most abundant neurons in the CNS. They facilitate communication between sensory and motor neurons and are integral to complex functions such as learning, memory, and decision-making.
Motor neurons transmit signals from the CNS to muscles, enabling movement. They are significantly less abundant than interneurons, constituting about 1% of the total neuron population in the CNS.

Neuroglial cells are supportive cells in the CNS, not neurons. While they outnumber neurons by about 10 to 1, they do not directly participate in neuronal signaling, so they cannot be classified as neurons.

questions.explanation: Interneurons (associative neurons) are the most abundant. neurons in the CNS. They process and relay information between sensory and motor neurons and are crucial for higher-order functions like learning, memory, and decision-making.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a part of neuron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The axon is a long, thin fiber that carries electrical impulses away from the neuron's cell body, playing a crucial role in transmitting signals. It is definitely a part of a neuron.
Dendrites are short, branching extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons. They are essential components of neurons.
Nissl granules are clusters of ribosomes found within the cell body of a neuron, involved in the synthesis of proteins. They are part of the neuron structure.
The synapse is the junction where two neurons communicate, involving the transmission of signals. However, it is not a structural component of a single neuron, making it the correct answer here.

questions.explanation: A synapse is the junction between two neurons for signal transmission and is not part of the neuron itself. Axons. dendrites, and Nissl granules are structural components of a neuron.

questions.text: _ is considered as chief structural and functional unit of nervous system.

options.explanation: The term 'cell' is too general. While it encompasses various types of cells, it does not specify the specialized cell type crucial for the nervous system's function.
Neurons are indeed the chief structural and functional units of the nervous system, responsible for transmitting signals and processing information.
Nephrons are the functional units of the kidney, not the nervous system, and therefore do not relate to this question.
While the brain is a critical component of the nervous system, it is not the only structure involved. Neurons, which are found throughout the nervous system, are the fundamental units.

questions.explanation: The neuron is the chief structural and functional unit of the nervous system. It transmits information through electrical and chemical signals and is responsible for sensations, thoughts, and movements.

questions.text: Which one of the following is common to all neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A cell body which contains a nucleus
options.explanation: The cell body, or soma, is crucial for all neurons as it contains the nucleus, which holds the genetic material and regulates cellular functions. This structure is key for neuronal maintenance and growth, making it present in every neuron.

Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath that are found in myelinated neurons, allowing for faster signal transmission. However, not all neurons are myelinated, and thus, some do not have these nodes, making this option incorrect.

Myelin sheaths insulate axons and enhance the speed of impulse conduction. However, the thickness of the myelin sheath varies among neurons, and some are unmyelinated. Therefore, this characteristic is not universal among all neurons.

Schwann cells are responsible for the formation of the myelin sheath around peripheral nerves. While they play a critical role in myelination, not all neurons are associated with Schwann cells, particularly those in the central nervous system, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The cell body (soma) is the main nutritional and metabolic center of all neurons, containing the nucleus and responsible for biosynthesis of proteins and energy for neuronal function.

questions.text: Which trait is not sex-linked recessive:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Haemophilia is a sex-linked recessive disorder, primarily affecting males, because the gene causing haemophilia is located on the X chromosome.
Colour blindness, particularly red-green colour blindness, is a sex-linked recessive trait found on the X chromosome.
This is correct answer as hypophosphatemia is an sex-linked dominant disease not recessive.
Testicular feminization syndrome (TFM), also known as Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome, is a sex-linked recessive disorder. It is caused by mutations in the androgen receptor gene on the X chromosome.

questions.explanation: This is correct answer as hypophosphatemia is an sex-linked dominant disease not recessive.

questions.text: “Law of independent assortment” states:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: That each pair of alleles assort independently of other pairs of alleles during gamete formation
options.explanation: This statement accurately represents the Law of Independent Assortment. It means that genes located on different chromosomes are inherited independently of each other during gamete formation.

options.text: That alleles of each pair of contrasting trait have unequal probability to assort with the alleles of other pair
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. The Law of Independent Assortment states that alleles segregate independently, regardless of the alleles at other gene loci. The probability of assorting is equal for all alleles.

options.text: That the two coexisting alleles for each trait segregate (separate) from each other at meiosis so that each gamete receives only one of the two alleles
options.explanation: This statement describes the Law of Segregation, not the Law of Independent Assortment. The Law of Segregation states that each individual has two alleles for each gene, and these alleles segregate during the formation of gametes.

options.text: That pertain to inheritance of single trait (monohybrid cross)
options.explanation: The Law of Independent Assortment is not specific to a single trait but applies to genes located on different chromosomes. The term "monohybrid cross" is more closely associated with the Law of Segregation.

questions.explanation: Mendel's law of independent assortment states that the alleles of two (or more) different genes get sorted into gametes independently of one another. In other words, the allele a gamete receives for one gene does not influence the allele received for another gene.

questions.text: In 'Complete Dominance':
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Different alleles of a gene are both expressed in heterozygous condition
options.explanation: This describes codominance, not complete dominance.In codominance, both alleles in a heterozygote are fully expressed — for example, in human AB blood group, both A and B alleles are expressed equally.However, in complete dominance, only one allele (the dominant one) shows its effect, while the recessive allele is completely masked.

options.text: One allele (R) is completely dominant over the other (r)
options.explanation: This is the definition of complete dominance.In this case, the dominant allele (R) completely masks the expression of the recessive allele (r) in a heterozygous individual (Rr).Example:In pea plants, the allele for round seeds (R) is dominant over wrinkled seeds (r).So both RR and Rr genotypes result in round seeds.

options.text: The phenotype of the heterozygote is intermediate between phenotypes of the two homozygotes
options.explanation: This describes incomplete dominance, not complete dominance.In incomplete dominance, neither allele is completely dominant; the heterozygous phenotype is a blend of both alleles.Example:In snapdragon flowers, a red (RR) and white (rr) parent produce pink (Rr) offspring.

options.text: Gene mutations may produce many different alleles of a gene
options.explanation: While gene mutations can indeed create multiple alleles, this has no relation to the concept of complete dominance.Complete dominance only describes how two existing alleles interact, not how alleles are formed through mutation.

questions.explanation: Complete dominance is a form of dominance in a heterozygous condition wherein the allele that is regarded as dominant completely masks the effect of the allele that is recessive. Option C refers to 'Incomplete Dominance' where a dominant allele, or form of a gene, does not completely mask the effects of a recessive allele, and the organism's resulting physical appearance shows a blending of both alleles.

questions.text: The genotype of blood group 'AB' in humans is _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the human blood group system, there are four main types: A, B, AB, and O. Each individual inherits two alleles for the ABO blood group, one from each parent. The A and B alleles are codominant, meaning that both are expressed when they are both present in an individual's genotype. The O allele is recessive, so it is only expressed when an individual has two copies of the O allele. The genotype of blood group AB is IAIB, which means that the individual has inherited one A allele and one B allele from their parents. These two alleles are codominant, so both the A and B antigens are expressed on the surface of their red blood cells. Individuals with blood group AB are considered universal recipients because they can receive blood from individuals with any blood group without causing a transfusion reaction. However, they can only donate blood to individuals with blood group AB.

questions.text: The total number of genes present in a particular population, at a particular time is called:

options.explanation: A gene is a specific segment of DNA that contains the instructions for building a particular protein or determining a specific trait. However, the total number of genes present in a population is not referred to as a "gene."

The genome refers to the complete set of genetic material (DNA) present in an organism. It includes all the genes, non-coding sequences, and regulatory elements. The genome encompasses the entire genetic information of an individual or a species.

Genotype refers to the genetic constitution of an individual, specifically the combination of alleles present in an organism's DNA. It represents the genetic information that determines the observable traits (phenotype) of an organism. However, the term "genotype" does not describe the total number of genes in a population.

Gene pool refers to the sum total of all the genes, alleles, and their variations present in a population at a particular time. It includes all the genetic information and diversity available within a population. The gene pool of a population is subject to change due to various factors such as mutation, migration, genetic drift, and natural selection.

A karyotype is a visual representation of the chromosomes of an individual or a species, arranged in a specific order. It allows for examining the number, structure, and size of chromosomes. While a karyotype provides valuable information about the organization of chromosomes, it does not describe the total number of genes in a population.

questions.text: Minimum energy required to eject an electron from metal surface is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Stopping potential refers to the voltage that must be applied to stop the emission of photoelectrons in a photoelectric experiment. It's not specifically related to the minimum energy required to eject an electron from a metal surface.

This is the correct option. The work function is the minimum amount of energy required to remove an electron from the surface of a metal, often denoted by the symbol Φ. questions.text: If frequency of incident light is increased beyond threshold frequency, keeping light intensity constant, then:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the frequency of incident light is increased beyond the threshold frequency, while keeping the light intensity constant, the photoelectric effect will still occur, but the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons will increase.The photoelectric effect is the phenomenon where electrons are emitted from a material when it is exposed to light of sufficient energy. Each material has a specific threshold frequency, also known as the cutoff frequency, below which no electrons are emitted regardless of the intensity of the light. When the frequency of the incident light exceeds the threshold frequency, electrons are ejected from the material. The energy of the emitted electrons depends on the frequency of the incident light. According to the equation E = hf, where E is the energy of a photon, h is Planck's constant, and f is the frequency of the light, the energy of the emitted electrons is directly proportional to the frequency of the incident light.Therefore, if the frequency of the incident light is increased beyond the threshold frequency, the energy of the photons increases, leading to higher kinetic energy of the emitted electrons. The number of emitted electrons remains proportional to the intensity of the incident light, as long as the intensity is above the threshold intensity.In summary, increasing the frequency of incident light beyond the threshold frequency, while keeping the light intensity constant, results in higher kinetic energy for the emitted electrons in the photoelectric effect.

questions.text: The uncertainty in momentum and position is due to its:

options.explanation: This option refers to the inherent uncertainty principle proposed by Werner Heisenberg, known as the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle. According to this principle, there is a fundamental limit to the precision with which certain pairs of physical properties, such as momentum and position, can be simultaneously known. So, this option correctly identifies the source of uncertainty in momentum and position.
Uncertainty in momentum and position is not inherently linked to two-dimensional motion. The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle applies to particles in all dimensions of motion.

While extreme velocities can certainly affect our ability to measure momentum and position accurately, the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle is a more fundamental explanation for the inherent uncertainty in these properties.

questions.explanation: In order to find the position and momentum of matter, we need to observe these metrics. In order to observe them, we need to have radiation reflect off of the matter after interacting with it. Hence to precisely know the measurements, we first must precisely know the nature of the radiation (wavelength and energy). However, in order to know the nature of radiation, we need it to interact with matter. Thus this juxtaposition stemming from the inherent nature of matter and radiation gives rise to the uncertainty principle.

Green light has a wavelength of about 550 nanometers. This is shorter than the wavelength of red light, so the momentum of a green photon is greater than the momentum of a red photon.

Blue light has a wavelength of about 400 nanometers. This is the shortest wavelength of all visible light, so the momentum of a blue photon is the greatest of all visible light photons.

questions.text: The number of photoelectrons emitted from metal depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The intensity of incident light refers to the brightness or the amount of light energy falling on the metal surface per unit area per unit time. According to the photoelectric effect, the number of photoelectrons emitted from a metal surface depends directly on the intensity of the incident light. Higher-intensity light means more photons, thus more photoelectrons are emitted. However, the energy of individual photons remains the same.

The energy of the incident light (i.e., the energy carried by each photon) plays a significant role in the photoelectric effect. For photoelectrons to be emitted from the metal, the energy of the incident photons must be equal to or greater than the work function of the metal (the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the metal's surface). Only photons with sufficient energy can cause photoemission.

The wavelength of the incident light is directly related to its energy. Shorter wavelengths (higher energy photons) have a greater chance of causing photoemission compared to longer wavelengths with lower energies. The energy of a photon is directly proportional to the frequency of light and inversely proportional to its wavelength.

The frequency of the incident light determines the energy of the individual photons. Photons with higher frequencies (corresponding to shorter wavelengths) have more energy and are more likely to cause the emission of photoelectrons.

questions.text: Magnetic flux is maximum when angle between magnetic field and vector area is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At an angle of 0 degrees, the magnetic field lines are perfectly aligned with the area vector, resulting in maximum magnetic flux. This is because cos(0) = 1, maximizing the product of the magnetic induction and the area.
At an angle of 90 degrees, the magnetic field lines are perpendicular to the area vector, resulting in zero magnetic flux. This is because cos(90) = 0, meaning no flux passes through the area.

At an angle of 180 degrees, the magnetic field lines are again perfectly aligned, but in the opposite direction. This also results in maximum magnetic flux as cos(180) = -1, indicating that the direction of the flux is reversed but the magnitude remains the same as at 0 degrees.

This option is correct because both 0 degrees and 180 degrees yield maximum (but opposite direction) magnetic flux, as explained above. However, it is essential to note that while both angles provide maximum flux, they represent different directions of the magnetic field.

questions.explanation: Magnetic flux is calculated as the product of the magnetic field strength (B) and the area (ΔA) through which it passes, adjusted by the cosine of the angle (θ) between the magnetic field lines and the normal (perpendicular) to the area vector. The formula is given by: Flux = BΔA cos(θ). The cosine function reaches its maximum value of 1 at an angle of 0 degrees, indicating that the magnetic field is aligned with the area vector. However, at 180 degrees, the magnetic field is aligned in the opposite direction, leading to a maximum absolute value of flux, but with a negative sign due to the cosine function. Therefore, the correct answers are 0 degrees and 180 degrees, making Option A correct. Options B and D are incorrect because 90 degrees results in zero flux, and while Option C is correct, it does not account for the nature of the question's request for a single maximum angle. Hence, while A and C are maximized in terms of magnitude, they represent different directional scenarios.

questions.text: The SI unit of magnetic flux is Weber which is equivalent to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The unit NA/m is the unit of magnetic field strength, also known as magnetic field intensity or magnetic field intensity. It represents the SI unit for magnetic field strength and is measured in amperes per meter (A/m).

The unit NmA is not a recognized unit in the SI system. Newton-meter (Nm) is the SI unit of torque or moment of force, representing the product of a force (measured in newtons) and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (measured in meters). Milliampere (mA) is the SI unit for electrical current, representing one thousandth of an ampere. Combining the two units, NmA, would not have a specific meaning or representation in a recognized SI unit.

The unit Nm^-1A^-1 represents the reciprocal of the unit for electrical resistance, which is ohm (Ω). Reciprocal of ohm: (Ω)^-1 = Ω^-1 = Nm^-1A^-1 Therefore, Nm^-1A^-1 is the unit of electrical conductance, which is the measure of how easily electric current can flow through a material. It is commonly denoted by the unit siemens (S) in the SI system.

The SI unit of magnetic flux is indeed the Weber (Wb). The Weber is defined as the amount of magnetic flux that passes through a surface of one square meter (1 m²) perpendicular to a magnetic field of one Tesla (1 T). In terms of base SI units, one Weber is equivalent to one Tesla meter squared (T·m²).

questions.explanation: The bond length for a given pair of elements is a function of number and types of orbital overlaps. Single bonds, with their end-to-end overlap called sigma bonds are the longest (and also the weakest). A double bond consists of a sigma bond and a pi bond, the side to side overlap of p-orbitals. This moves the nuclei closer together and decreases the bond length (and increases the bond energy). A triple bond consists of two pi bonds and a sigma bond. The additional pi bond isn’t any shorter than the first pi bond, but together the two pi bonds provide additional attraction and shorten the sigma bond allowing the triple bond to be the shortest (and strongest).C−C ………. 154 pmC=C ………. 134 pmC≡C ………. 121 pm

questions.text: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes exhibits:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes exhibits addition reactions. Addition reactions in an unsaturated compound mean a compound that has a double bond can undergo addition reactions. The addition reactions can be classified into three main categories. Electrophilic addition: In these types of reactions, a species, that accepts an electron pair, attacks the electron-rich site of the double bond between carbon atoms. The reaction examples include the reactions of alkenes with hydrogen halides to give alkyl halides. Nucleophilic addition: In these types of reactions, species that have electron pairs, donate to form a chemical bond. These reactions usually occur in compounds having a carbonyl group like aldehydes or ketones. Free-radical addition: In these types of reactions, non-polar molecules form bonds with unsaturated compounds. These reactions occur when free radicals are formed. Free radicals are species that have unpaired valence electrons.

questions.text: Benzene can be prepared from:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Benzene can be derived from petroleum through processes such as catalytic reforming and steam cracking of naphtha. These methods involve refining lighter fractions of crude oil to yield benzene as a byproduct.
Coal tar, a byproduct of coal carbonization, contains benzene. Through fractional distillation, benzene and other aromatic compounds are separated from coal tar at specific temperature ranges.

Acetylene (ethyne) can be converted to benzene by cyclic polymerization. This process involves passing acetylene through a red-hot tube at high temperatures, leading to the formation of the benzene ring.
Benzene can be obtained from phenol by reducing it with zinc dust. The reaction involves the reduction of phenol to form benzene and zinc oxide as a byproduct.
All the listed methods can be used to produce benzene, showcasing the diverse chemical pathways available for benzene synthesis.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that benzene can be prepared from all the mentioned sources. Petroleum: Benzene is obtained through catalytic reforming and steam cracking of naphtha, which are standard industrial processes for hydrocarbon refinement. Coal: During the carbonization of coal, benzene is one of the volatile compounds distilled from coal tar. Acetylene: Acetylene undergoes cyclic polymerization at high temperatures, forming benzene as a result. Phenol: Reduction of phenol using zinc dust is another chemical method to synthesize benzene, with zinc oxide as a byproduct. Thus, each method demonstrates a valid approach to benzene production.

questions.text: The electrophilic reactions of Benzene are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: During halogenation, benzene reacts with halogens, such as chlorine or bromine, in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst like FeCl3 or FeBr3, to form aryl halides. This is a classic electrophilic substitution reaction where the hydrogen atom is replaced by a halogen atom.

Nitration involves the reaction of benzene with concentrated nitric acid and sulfuric acid at 323-333 K, forming nitrobenzene. An electrophile, the nitronium ion (NO2+), replaces a hydrogen atom in benzene.

Sulphonation is the process where benzene is heated with fuming sulfuric acid (a mixture of H2SO4 and SO3), resulting in benzene sulfonic acid. This reaction is reversible and involves the substitution of a hydrogen atom by a sulfonyl group (SO3H).

In Friedel-Crafts alkylation and acylation, benzene reacts with alkyl or acyl chlorides in the presence of a catalyst like AlCl3. These reactions involve the replacement of a hydrogen atom with an alkyl or acyl group, respectively.
This option is correct as all the reactions listed—halogenation, nitration, sulfonation, alkylation, and acylation—are electrophilic substitution reactions of benzene.

questions.explanation: Electrophilic substitution reactions of benzene involve the replacement of a hydrogen atom on the benzene ring with an electrophile. questions.text: In the reaction, R-C≡C-R → the reagent used to convert alkyne into cis alkene is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hydrogen in the presence of catalysts such as Pd, Pt, and Ni causes full reduction. Alkynes are fully hydrogenated into alkanes and the alkene intermediate formed during the process (by the addition of hydrogen to alkyne) can’t be isolated.

To convert an alkyne into a cis alkene, the reagent commonly used is Lindlar's catalyst. Lindlar's catalyst consists of palladium (Pd) deposited on calcium carbonate (CaCO3) or barium sulfate (BaSO4) support, with quinoline or lead acetate as a poison to prevent further reduction to an alkane. Lindlar's catalyst selectively reduces the triple bond of an alkyne to a cis-alkene or trans-alkene, depending on the reaction conditions. By using Lindlar's catalyst under appropriate conditions (such as low temperature and low hydrogen pressure), cis-alkenes can be obtained. The reaction mechanism involves the partial reduction of the alkyne, stopping at the alkene stage. The presence of the poison helps to hinder the complete reduction to an alkane and directs the selectivity towards the cis-alkene product. Therefore, Lindlar's catalyst is the reagent commonly employed to convert an alkyne into a cis alkene.

The general formula for the aldehyde group is R-CHO or R-COH. The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde with the formula HCOH.Aldehydes and ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) as a functional group. A ketone has two alkyl or aryl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (RCOR’). The simplest ketone is acetone, which has two methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (CH3COCH3).An aldehyde is similar to a ketone, except that instead of two side groups connected to the carbonyl carbon, they have at least one hydrogen (RCOH or R-CHO). The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde (HCOH), as it has two hydrogens connected to the carbonyl group. questions.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) as a functional group. A ketone has two alkyl or aryl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (RCOR’). The simplest ketone is acetone, which has two methyl groups attached to the carbonyl carbon (CH3COCH3).An aldehyde is similar to a ketone, except that instead of two side groups connected to the carbonyl carbon, they have at least one hydrogen (RCOH or R-CHO). The simplest aldehyde is formaldehyde (HCOH), as it has two hydrogens connected to the carbonyl group. All other aldehydes have one hydrogen bonded to the carbonyl group, like the simple molecule acetaldehyde, which has one hydrogen and one methyl group (HCOCH3).The carbonyl carbon in both aldehydes and ketones is electrophilic, meaning that it has a dipole due to the electronegativity of the attached oxygen atom. This makes the carbonyl carbon an ideal target for nucleophiles in a nucleophilic addition reaction. During this reaction, the nucleophile, or electron donor, attacks the carbonyl to form the tetrahedral intermediate. The negatively charged oxygen accepts a hydrogen ion to form a hydroxyl group.Typically, nucleophiles possess a negative charge or lone pair on a heteroatom, which can take several forms (OH-, RO-, CN-, R3C-, RNH2, ROH). For primary amines (RNH2), the reaction does not stop at the formation of the tetrahedral intermediate with a hydroxyl group. Rather, an elimination reaction occurs that produces a double-bonded carbon and nitrogen functional group known as an imine. Understanding the reactions that aldehydes and ketones can undergo provides a way to differentiate between these similar organic compounds.
